text.title: Google AI Overviews Optimization Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules every Google AI Overviews Optimization application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them. Plus a skip-methodology rule for decision-tree leaves that exit the methodology.

rule.statement: Place a 1-2 sentence direct answer immediately after each section heading. Heading-only sections without an answer block = REJECT.
rule.rationale: AIO extracts the first concise answer; without it, the citation goes to a competitor.

rule.statement: Every page implements Article schema + FAQ schema where Q&A patterns exist. Missing FAQ schema on FAQ pages = REJECT.
rule.rationale: AIO relies on structured data for fast, confident citation.

rule.statement: Pages targeting AIO queries are updated within 30 days of publish OR flagged for refresh. >60 days without update = REJECT.
rule.rationale: AIO favours recent content; stale pages drop out of citation.

rule.statement: Named entities (products, authors, organisations) use the same canonical form across the cluster + match the structured data.
rule.rationale: AIO reconciles multiple sources; drift creates ambiguity that lowers ranking.

rule.statement: Author byline carries machine-readable credentials (sameAs links to LinkedIn / GitHub / Google Scholar). Anonymous authors = REJECT for AIO target pages.
rule.rationale: Author authority is a key citation signal.

rule.statement: AIO impressions are tracked in GSC. Without tracking, optimisation is folklore.
Skip this methodology when the decision tree resolves to a skip branch — preconditions are not met OR an adjacent methodology fits better.
rule.rationale: Skipping cleanly preserves the integrity of the rubric; misapplied methodologies produce false positives downstream.
